forecaster_note: Tue 21 Jul — the E swell is still the show: 5.5ft @ 10s from 71°, right in your 30–80° sweet spot, with a small 2.4ft @ 11s S line (171°) underneath. Energy ~371 kJ through the morning, consistency 100%. Wind is the clock today: light WNW/NW offshore at 6 kph until about 9am, cross-shore N by 10am, then NE onshore 12 kph at midday building to 16 kph through the afternoon. Low tide 7:07am, high 1:44pm. Water 17.2°C, air topping ~18°C. Get out early or don't bother. — Surf Guru read on live Surfline data

wind.summary: Light NW offshore 6 kph to about 9am, cross-shore N by 10am, then NE onshore 12 kph at midday and 16 kph by 2pm. Clean window is dawn–10am.

shark.summary: No shark detections, sightings or drumline alerts logged for the Northern Beaches (Palm Beach to Manly) in the last 24 hours. SharkSmart drone patrols listed as operating along the coast. Most recent NSW activity was well away — a White Shark tagged and released at Shellharbour and drone sightings around Newcastle.

days.label: Today
days.note: Genuinely good early window on a Tuesday — 5.5ft/10s ENE swell at 71°, 371 kJ, 100% consistency, under a light NW offshore that only lasts until about 9–10am. Long Reef is holding the most size (head to 2ft overhead); Dee Why Point and Curl Curl are the cleaner, more manageable options. Everything shuts down once the NE onshore fills in from midday.

dee-why.window: Dawn to 10am. Low tide 7:07am has the banks shallow and punchy first up; the push toward the 1:44pm high gives the Point rights a longer wall but softens the beachies. Wind goes cross at 10 and NE onshore by midday — out of the water by then.

tip.label: Frontside tip
tip.text: 71° is the angle the Point wants — no dead-S wrap problem today, and 100% consistency means you won't sit waiting. Sit wide on the rights and let the 10s period stack them; 371 kJ is moderate, so keep your rail engaged rather than expecting it to throw for you.

long-reef.window: Biggest of the four again — the reef takes the full ENE energy and the NW offshore holds it open until about 9:30am. Best on the incoming after the 7:07am low. Step-up territory if the sets are running head-high plus.

tip.label: Frontside tip
tip.text: Rights off the reef with real weight behind them. It'll feel a size up on Dee Why because it is — commit early on the drop and use the offshore to hold your line through the first section.

curl-curl.window: Clean shifting peaks under the NW offshore, chest to overhead. Same early window — in by 7, out by 10. High wave count with consistency at 100%.

tip.label: Backside tip
tip.text: The ENE angle makes the lefts here run better than usual. Pick the peak with sand behind it and drive off the bottom early on your backhand rather than stalling for the pocket.

alerts: 💨 Surfline flags the NNW as onshore at North Steyne this morning — expect a touch more texture than the beaches to the north.
manly.window: Decent size at North Steyne but the wind angle is less favourable here than at Dee Why or Curl Curl. Fallback option if the northern spots are crowded, or an easy paddle before work.

tip.label: Backside tip
tip.text: Straight ENE swell lights up the whole stretch — walk until you find a bank peeling rather than settling for the first close-out in front of the car.

days.label: Tomorrow
days.note: Swell more than halves — energy drops to ~164 kJ as the ENE pulse fades to 4.3ft @ 10s from 66°. Still clean under a WNW offshore (9 kph, gusts 17) all morning, so it's a soft but tidy session rather than a powerful one. Long Reef keeps the most on offer.

dee-why.window: WNW offshore holds all morning — clean but noticeably weaker than Tuesday. Fun waist-to-chest on the Point; a step up in volume under your feet won't hurt.

long-reef.window: Holds the most residual energy again and rates best of the four. Cleanest before 10am off the reef — the pick if you want size on a fading swell.

curl-curl.window: Tidy waist-to-chest peaks all morning under the offshore. Easy, high-wave-count session — good for working on flow rather than power surfing.

manly.window: Clean and modest with the WNW offshore. Fuller-volume board weather — the smallest, softest of the four.

days.label: Weekend
days.note: A big S groundswell lands — energy jumps to 854 kJ Saturday and 1089 kJ Sunday, the most raw power of the run. The problem is the angle: 7.1ft @ 12s from 186° Saturday and 6.8ft @ 11s from 182° Sunday is effectively dead south, which wraps badly into Dee Why and is largely shadowed at Manly. Long Reef and Curl Curl are the S-exposed picks. Saturday morning carries a stiff W offshore (16 kph gusting 33); Sunday is lighter at 12 kph but the wind tips cross-shore at the reef.

alerts: ⚠️ Dead-S swell (182–186°) — big energy on paper but it wraps poorly into Dee Why. Expect less size than the kJ figure suggests.
💨 Sat morning W gusting to 33 kph — clean but hard work.
dee-why.window: Both mornings run offshore. Saturday is the cleaner-angled of the two here; Sunday has more raw energy but drops to 75% consistency, so expect longer waits between sets. Don't be fooled by the kJ — the angle kills most of it at the Point.

alerts: 💨 Sat morning W gusting to 33 kph — expect chop on the faces despite being offshore.
⚠️ Sunday wind swings cross-shore at the reef — Saturday is the cleaner call here.
long-reef.window: Best-exposed to the S swell and the standout both days — 2ft overhead and holding. Saturday 8–11am under the W offshore is the session to plan for. Step-up board, and treat it with respect at this size.

curl-curl.window: Takes the S swell far better than Dee Why — solid overhead peaks with the offshore holding them open. Saturday morning rates cleanest before the Sunday wind turns cross.

alerts: ⚠️ S swell is largely shadowed by the southern headland — smallest of the four despite the big energy figure.
manly.window: Clean but shadowed. Only worth it if the bigger spots are too much, or you want an easy paddle.
